moment_text: So I have, uh, two inflection points that really stood out to me. Uh, when I thought about this question, the first was a very long time ago. And I was very upset when the line crown that I good with brotherhood was taught to us over and over, and it's kind of apropos under the sisterhood because I was really upset about why is it brotherhood when they mean everyone? Why isn't it sisterhood or personhood at, you know, five years old? I was outraged about this. And so at a very young age. I recognized o or others recognized in me this sense of fairness and social justice and what was right. And I definitely at that time did have an awareness that some people had more power than others.

moment_text: That, you know, the, the playing field was not even, and that really got me upset at that young age, many years later. And there was one course, it was co-taught by a social worker and an educator, and every paper or essay that I submitted came back with. Furious notes about have you ever thought about social work? Do you would make a great social worker? And I, I never really did think about social work or, or understanding the field, but it was at that time that I really began to understand how someone's environment and environmental factors really impact their trajectory, uh, our trajectory and our way of achievement. Certainly physical health, mental health. That access to opportunities and resources could really, really impact someone's life. That really was exciting to me and so decided I was gonna go for it.

moment_text: And when I was, uh, doing my graduate work studying social work, um, I was placed as an intern in domestic abuse services, which was a field that I hadn't really given much thought to. moment_text: And so. Just sort of really dug in and. 10 years into it became the director, which was amazing. moment_text: Yeah, sure. So I'll, I'll share a story that, um, I often share and, and when I did this, this work, uh, leading the agency, I did a lot of, uh, community events and, and speaking to people who didn't know very much about domestic violence or so they thought. So I like to tell this story that imagine you're having a picnic on the side, uh, you know, in the woods on the, on the side of a river, and you notice that there as the river's flowing, that there are people. Slowing down the river who are having a hard time and drowning and needing help. And so you put your sandwich down and you go to the river and you help this person get out and make sure they're safe, and you give them a blanket and a few minutes later, someone else comes down the river and you go and you help them out. And this continues through the afternoon where you're just fishing people out of the water. And eventually you think, you know what? I'm gonna take a walk to the top of the hill and find out why people are falling in in the first place.

moment_text: And so what I realized is that we're always gonna take care of people who need help. We're always going to fish those people out, support them, help them get what they need. The the first initially is primary prevention, which means really stopping it before it starts. So we launched programs directed at young people at the high school level teaching about teen dating violence. Teaching about healthy relationships, eye-opening, uh, concepts to talk about with teenagers who are so in the moment. Um, I always like to think of them as little amplifiers, whatever. moment_text: So, you know, this is the most important relationship. I'll never love this person ever again. And then even at the younger ages, starting as young as kindergarten, we were teaching kids about. Communication, feelings, identification, empathy, uh, conflict negotiation, right? So yeah, you might be on the playground and someone has the swing that you want and that could feel really upsetting and you could be really, really angry and that's okay. But are you gonna go and push them off the swing and take it? Right? So teaching these nuances to children. And then at the middle school level also, when relationships. Start to change as friendships into more romantic relationships, but even healthy relationships within a friend, excuse me, within a friendship, understanding boundaries and consent. moment_text: And the, the last part of of prevention or another part of prevention that we really focused on 90% of domestic violence is perpetrated by men and saying that. Most men are not violent. What's happening with, with men that they are, um, perpetrating these kinds of behaviors. When we think about domestic violence, it's such a gendered issue. We usually think, oh, it's a woman's issue. This is a women's problem. And yes, women are certainly impacted, and we need to look at what's going on with men and try to change fundamental attitudes, beliefs, what socially acceptable behavior. So, as I said, 90% of men are not abusive, are not violent. So let's say one out of 10 men are that one abuser relies on the silence and sort of. Unspoken consent of the other nine men. So as we know, domestic violence lives in the shadows. It lives in secret, and so it takes other people, and in this case that the example I'm talking about right now with social capital, with influence to be able to call out. Behaviors. And that doesn't necessarily only mean intervening when something really terrible is happening in the extreme, but derogatory comments, sexist jokes, these kinds of things.

moment_text: And it doesn't also mean that someone needs to be, you know, a superhero running around with a cape. I'm reminded as I'm talking to you now of an initiative that we did that was, I was very, very proud of, which was working with the high school football team in our town, and the head coach really understood the, the social capital and the power that he as a head football coach had in a small suburban town and also his players and saw. Teaching these these players in a way that added to their concept of leadership. And so he dedicated time during their football practice for myself and colleagues to come in and talk to these young boys about the, the role that they can play, not to wag our fingers at them and to say, don't be bad boys, but to help them understand the power that they could have as being. What we called an upstander instead of a bystander, to really, um, set the norm for what's socially acceptable in a positive way.

moment_text: And the most important reason, which I think is often not always acknowledged and hard to understand, is that. Victims often love their abusers. There's a connection to this person. There could be real love underneath, and it's very hard to leave to hold someone accountable. Certainly call the police when it's somebody that you love.

moment_text: One thing I thought I'd share in, in talking about this is, is the metaphor of the frog in the pot. Um, so many people will look or at a situation and look at a victim and say, how could she. Let herself get in this situation, why does she stay in that situation? And so domestic violence can very often be like a frog in a pot, which I guess this is really true. If you were to take a pot of boiling water and put a baby frog or. Grownup frog in the pot, they would recognize the water's hot and they would jump right out if you were to take a pot of cold water and put a baby frog in the cold water and slowly turn up the heat. Frog doesn't realize that the water is getting hotter and hotter and will not jump out and will often stay till they're burned out. And so this analogy is really important in domestic violence because it is insidious. Nobody goes out on a first date with someone and is called a jerk, or a loser or an idiot, and then decides, oh, I think I'm gonna keep seeing this person. It doesn't start out that way. These behaviors develop slowly over time, and before you know it, you're in the hot water.

This is something, it becomes a part of you. It becomes a part of the way you speak and the way you move through the world. moment_text: We spent a lot of time supporting our each other in this work. And doesn't she realize? And you could go lean up in the doorway of a colleague and say, do you have a minute? I just need to. I just need to put this somewhere. It didn't help. It wasn't the worst thing in the world that we were all trained in, in the social services field also. So we knew how to support each other, but to be able to create time and space for those moments to keep ourselves going, to keep each other going. We, during COVID. Was really interesting time to do this work, um, when we were all at home operating two hotlines through a network of cell phones and a, a shelter and, you know, still maintaining to do all of the work that we had always done. And during that time especially, it became so clear how important it was for us to stay connected to each other. We had our ongoing individual support meetings, our ongoing weekly, sometimes twice a week. All staff meetings, and we always took time to, of course, cover the bases, go through our agenda, do the work we needed to do and save space. That was just about connection on a human level. A colleague of mine invented what she called the she share. We started pretty much every meeting with a she share and she being so creative came up with all kinds of fun prompts. Like, who was your first celebrity crush? You know, how early can you remember? And we'd just be silly together, connect on that human level to be able to put on our suits of armor and go back to the work. So that was a really important piece of it.

moment_text: Boundaries is a big trigger word in our field. We have to maintain our boundaries. We have our code of ethics. Um, and there is some debate in the field about sort of abandoning the traditional Freudian blank slate relationship or lack of, with, with a client that you're working with. I think a more contemporary view is recognizing that it's okay to be a human. With your clients, it's okay to share a laugh. You know, you don't wanna share something that it's, whatever you do share, it has to be in the best interest of your client, right? Your client isn't there to meet your needs of connection. It's really about maintaining, um, the view of serving them. But more and more research is pointing to the fact that the most effective. Part of a therapy of therapy is the therapeutic relationship. So there are a lot of modalities out there now that are evidence-based and amazing and, you know, can really help people. And given all of those modalities, the most important piece is the human connection.

moment_text: Absolutely. Meeting all different kinds of people with different kinds of challenges, but the one common thread is almost everyone is coming because there's some kind of challenge in important relationship. It's always about the relationship and just like you said, whether it's a, a partner. A child, an adult child, a parent, a brother. I mean, that's what makes us human right, is how we relate to each other. And none of this happens in a vacuum. And if there is conflict or discord in one relationship, there's no way that it doesn't impact other areas of our lives. moment_text: I love the language of marching. Um, for me, mental wellness, because it is. It, it's something that's ongoing, right? It's the active verb of it. It's not how did you do this? You know, these concepts that we're trying to support other people with for ourselves, and believe me, none of us have it together. None of us have it. Right? Which is again, brings me that it's ongoing, but to be mindful. I think it is really to me, the core of what all of this is about, to be deliberate, um, to be aware. And I have learned this from my colleagues and, and mentors. You know, we can't control what happens around us. We can't, um, we can't ignore it. Uh, we can't take it on all the time, but if we can. moment_text: We have to look at it, we have to face it, and then we get to decide what we wanna do. So, so having choice, recognizing that pretty much everything we do is choice. moment_text: Well, thank you for that. moment_text: That's a hard one because my first instinct is to say, who am I to give a message? We all have a message. We all have something to contribute. We all have faults and uh, we all have strengths. And so, you know, I think for me, one of the most important pieces is to recognize the strength, recognize the faults, and accept acceptance. Accept what we can control. Let go of what we can't, doesn't mean we have to be happy about it. Right? Acceptance doesn't mean that you're, is not the same thing as approval, right? So we accept that this is, this is what is, and what can I do about it? How can I continue in a way that's aligned in. With my internal moral compass, with what I wanna achieve with my boundaries, that being really deliberate in how we move through the world. I think it takes work. That would be my message is to be very mindful and aware of how you move through the world and, and make choice. You get to decide. How do you move through the world?
